title: Pinpointing Urgency and Running Effective Weekly Team Meetings
summary: Erin Stetzer taught a five-step framework for pinpointing and responding to urgent situations, emphasizing that making a decision is the hardest but most critical step. She then covered how and why to run weekly team focus meetings, including structure, purpose, and breakout sessions. The session opened to Q&A where participants discussed client communication boundaries, PM accountability scorecards, micro-team cooperation, and challenges finding strong project managers.

teachings.explanation: When situations arise, Erin uses a five-step process: (1) Recognize it honestly, (2) Make a decision on how to move forward, (3) Commit to that decision, (4) Make a plan, (5) Execute the plan. The key insight is that once the decision is made, the hardest part is over and everything else becomes a straightforward sequence of actions.
teachings.quote: Once you make that decision, the hard lift is over. You're not going back.

teachings.explanation: The act of making a clear decision — even a painful or costly one — is what removes paralysis and enables forward momentum. Avoiding or delaying decisions is what makes situations feel complicated and overwhelming.
teachings.quote: I think the world becomes complicated when we kind of hide from making a decision.

teachings.concept: Urgent vs. Not Urgent Bucket System
teachings.explanation: Not everything requires immediate action. Items that are important but not urgent should be placed into organized 'buckets' by category — per project manager, per project, marketing, new business, client attention — so they are captured and addressed systematically rather than lost or ignored.
teachings.quote: They're not so urgent, but they're really important. Okay. So that's part one, pinpointing urgency.

teachings.concept: Weekly Team Meetings for Camaraderie and Bottleneck Clearing
teachings.explanation: As teams grow beyond five or six people, a weekly meeting becomes essential — not just for logistics, but for human connection. The meeting structure includes casual rapport-building, surfacing where people are stuck, discussing shared resources and trade relationships, and ending with focused breakout sessions between relevant parties.
teachings.quote: We're so busy running in different lanes all week long that we really don't get to kind of laugh a little bit with each other or goof around a little bit or find out what someone might be needing help with.

teachings.explanation: In construction scenarios like a post-warranty water leak, Erin demonstrates that committing to resolve an issue before knowing its full cost or scope is what enables effective action. The commitment itself unlocks the planning and execution steps.
teachings.quote: I don't know if this is going to cost me $10,000. I don't know if this is going to cost me $50,000. All I know is I'm committed.

teachings.concept: People, Time, Money, Results, and Quality as Priority Filter
teachings.explanation: When pulled in multiple directions throughout a day, Erin filters every incoming issue through five lenses: people, time, money, results, and quality. If an issue threatens to stop progress in any of those areas, it gets prioritized immediately or delegated. Everything else waits.
teachings.quote: Any incoming problem, situation, whatever it is, if it affects — we'll stop any of those dead in our tracks — people, time, money, results, and quality, I will make time for that.

teachings.explanation: Erin runs her day by letting her calendar dictate her schedule rather than reacting to incoming demands. She communicates a clear response time to clients and team members, which reduces urgency anxiety without abandoning responsiveness.
teachings.quote: The most important thing that I learned is my calendar runs the show.

teachings.explanation: Erin earns her team's respect not by having all the answers, but by openly admitting when she doesn't know something and inviting collaborative problem-solving. This creates a culture of mutual respect and psychological safety rather than a top-down authority dynamic.
teachings.quote: When she doesn't have an answer, she doesn't act like she has an answer. She's very open to, hey, what do y'all think? What have you seen? What are your ideas?

teachings.explanation: Different internal teams (project managers and money managers) are kept aligned through structured, recurring touchpoints built into shared workflows — invoice review folders, monthly draw preparation huddles, and modification logs — so collaboration is systematized rather than ad hoc.
teachings.quote: There are little mini practices that they have together that are set up in systems so that they do interact with each other in a very simple processed way.

actionable_advice: Use the five-step urgency framework — recognize, decide, commit, plan, execute — to process incoming issues rather than letting them pile up or stall.
Organize non-urgent but important items into named buckets (by project manager, project, marketing, etc.) to ensure nothing is lost while preserving focus on urgent matters.
Structure weekly team meetings to include: casual rapport-building, bottleneck identification, resource and trade discussion, and breakout sessions for specific collaborators.
Block your calendar and communicate a specific callback time when you cannot address an issue immediately, rather than leaving people without a response.
Filter all incoming issues through the lens of people, time, money, results, and quality — if it threatens any of these, address it or delegate it now.
Visit job sites unannounced regularly and ask detailed questions to project managers to set standards, surface knowledge gaps, and signal what level of attention to detail is expected.

Hold quarterly one-on-one meetings with team members focused not just on their role performance but on identifying the 20% of work that uniquely energizes them and finding ways to build that into their role.
When a client complaint arises — even out of warranty — always take the call, gather information, and evaluate responsibility before deciding on a course of action.

Use a simple whiteboard check-in before weekly meetings where project managers self-report whether they completed their non-negotiables, to reduce administrative burden while maintaining accountability.
Consider using the Voxer app as a walkie-talkie-style communication tool for team members who struggle with texting or prefer voice communication.
client_challenges: Sandy asked how long after project completion a builder should still take responsibility for issues and step in to resolve them.
Lauren asked how to set client communication expectations upfront, including whether to give clients direct access to project managers and how to manage clients who text at all hours.
Julie asked how Erin addresses trending execution issues with her team and trades, and how she earns and maintains respect from her full team.

Janine asked for a more detailed explanation of how the PM scorecard works week to week and how to hold project managers accountable to non-negotiables without creating excessive administrative burden.
Walker asked about Erin's role within internal micro-teams and how sub-teams like project managers and money managers cooperate and communicate.

Denise shared an ongoing challenge finding a project manager who combines field expertise, broad project oversight, and strong communication skills — her current PM is experienced in the field but struggles with basic digital communication and providing proactive updates.
Renee asked how to set meeting expectations when different team members want or need different types of connection and support, and how to balance that variation.
notable_quotes: On decision-making: 'Once you make that decision, the hard lift is over. You're not going back.'
On avoidance: 'I think the world becomes complicated when we kind of hide from making a decision.'
On commitment under uncertainty: 'I don't know if this is going to cost me $10,000. I don't know if this is going to cost me $50,000. All I know is I'm committed.'
On prioritization: 'People, time, money, results, and quality — if it affects any of those, I will make time for that.'
On learning in construction: 'There's no textbook in building. That's legit. Because we all learn these things literally every single day.'

On team culture and systems: 'There was zero issue on our end about not performing. It was so seamless' — describing how two teammates stepped in flawlessly when a third had a family emergency two weeks before a closing.
